title: Automotive Paint Booth (High-Class Paint Booth)
content:
![Automotive Paint Booth (High-Class Paint Booth)](https://ysanona.com/products/1-1-3-kalux-series-high-class-paint-booth_02.webp)

Kalux Series

Automotive Paint Booth (High-Class Paint Booth)

Industrial spray booths for car refinishing

The Kalux series is the high-class option in this line of automotive paint booths. It has stronger airflow, a cleaner interior layout and better lighting, which makes the workspace more comfortable for technicians handling detailed refinishing work.

Downdraft automotive paint booths are useful for car refinishing because the air flows cleanly and directly from the ceiling to the floor. The air flows in one direction from the ceiling to the floor, which keeps the temperature more stable and makes it easier to paint and cure. Thanks to the airflow moving downward, overspray and solvent vapors leave the paint booth faster, which keeps the painted surface clean and makes the area safer for the operators. Side-draft booths usually need more energy to reach the same result. A downdraft booth can use about 20–30% less because the heat stays in the area where the work is done instead of spreading around. The initial investment is higher, but the paint finish on the vehicle is more uniform, rework is reduced, and the technicians can move through the process with more ease.

title: Paint Booth Filter
content:
![Paint Booth Filter](https://ysanona.com/products/1-10-paint-booth-filter_01.webp)

Paint Booth Filter

Spray booth ceiling filter media for capturing fine dust and overspray particles in spray paint booths

Paint booth filters are used to capture fine dust and overspray inside spray booths, keeping the airflow clean and stable throughout the painting process. Ceiling filter media help distribute air evenly from the top of the booth, while floor or exhaust filters trap overspray before it leaves the workspace. Different filter types are available depending on the required air velocity, filtration level and booth configuration.

Common Types of Paint Booth Filter Media
 Paint booth filters are usually divided into different types depending on where they are installed and what part of the airflow they handle:

Ceiling filter media (high-efficiency filter layer)
 Installed under the plenum chamber and covering the full top area of the booth. This filter medium is the most important layer during the finishing process because it traps most of the overspray before it reaches the floor filters. Thanks to ceiling filters, the filtration accuracy is higher, usually ≥1–5 μm, and high-efficiency types can reach sub-micron levels. They absorb overspray well and usually need replacement after about 400 working hours. The ceiling filters condition has a direct influence on the final paint result, since worn or clogged filters affect airflow and can lead to uneven surfaces or particles settling on the finish.

Bag filters for intake or exhaust
 Bag filters for intake or exhaust have a much bigger filtering area than flat media, often several times larger for the same size. Because of this, they last 2–5 times longer before needing replacement. The unit price is higher, but the long service life reduces the yearly cost. They use a metal frame and tear-resistant synthetic fibers that stay stable under stronger airflow and do not deform easily. Their large dust-holding capacity, low pressure drop and long lifespan make them a practical option for spray booths, especially in high-load conditions where stricter emission standards are required. In general, their overall performance is better than traditional flat filters.

Intake filter media (pre-filters)
 Installed at the fresh-air inlet or in front of the intake fan. They capture larger particles, usually ≥5–10 μm, and hold a considerable amount of dust. These filters need to be replaced regularly, generally every 100 working hours or earlier if contamination increases.

Floor filter media (fiberglass filter / exhaust filter)
 Located under the floor grilles or before the exhaust fan. These filters trap fine overspray particles and dust that pass through the ceiling filter or rise from the floor. They require high filtration performance and have a large dust-holding capacity. Replacement is typically needed every 100 working hours.

Filter paper
 Made from natural fibers (pulp, cotton), synthetic fibers (polyester, polypropylene) or inorganic fibers (fiberglass). It uses interception and adsorption to separate impurities in dry spray booth systems. Special pleated filter paper is used as an alternative to water curtains, capturing overspray efficiently while remaining easy to handle and environmentally friendly.

Features

- Made with durable synthetic fibers that do not release particles into the airflow, even when the booth operates under strong vibration

- A gradient-density structure is created through layered fiber arrangements, allowing each layer to trap different particle sizes. Higher fiber density improves overall filtration performance

- Fully impregnated adhesive prevents dust from passing through, and the filtered air is distributed evenly across the ceiling area to support consistent paint quality.

- The outer surface has a dense finish reinforced with a grid mesh, giving the media good dust-holding capacity and a longer service life.

- Logos can be added directly to the filter media when required.

- Complies with DlN 53438-F1 and UL 900 Class 2 standards.

title: Bus & Truck Spray Booth
content:
![Bus & Truck Spray Booth](https://ysanona.com/products/1-2-bus-truck-spray-booth_01.webp)

Bus & Truck Spray Booth

Industrial spray booths for large vehicle finishing

When working on special-purpose vehicles, construction machinery or firefighting units, the coating process needs to match the size and shape of these vehicles. These vehicles are often very tall, very long or shaped in a non-standard way, so the airflow, heating and access points need to be set up to match their size. Open spraying areas and regular automotive booths usually don’t have enough height, booth length or airflow strength to deal with this type of work. Large spray booths designed for buses and trucks offer the space and control needed for these projects. Their extended booth length, reinforced airflow and higher heating capacity support consistent coating on long or uneven surfaces. Full-size doors and side access points also make it easier to reach different parts of the vehicle during both manufacturing and repair work. With the right heat distribution, the curing stage becomes more stable, which helps the coating last longer on vehicles that work outdoors or under heavy use.

Applications

- City buses

- Long-distance coaches

- Heavy-duty trucks

- Armored vehicles

- Military transport vehicles

- Subway cars

- Rail car shells

- Construction vehicles (excavators, cranes, etc.)

- Agricultural machinery (tractors, harvesters, etc.)

title: Aircraft Paint Booth
content:
![Aircraft Paint Booth](https://ysanona.com/products/1-3-aircraft-paint-booth_01.webp)

Aircraft Paint Booth

Industrial spray booths for aviation and aerospace finishing

Aircraft of different sizes and categories, commercial, military and private, require a controlled environment for paint application and curing work. An aircraft paint booth provides the stability needed for localized repairs, full-aircraft repainting and the application of specialized aerospace coatings.

Typical situations include :

- Repairs on areas affected by lightning, corrosion or structural fixes, where a clean and enclosed workspace helps the coating stick properly

- For small repairs on places such as the leading edges or the landing-gear doors, aircraft paint booths help to keep the temperature steady, so the paint dries evenly and looks the same as the rest of the surface

- When an aircraft needs full repaint during a major check or a livery update, it helps to work in a space where the airflow and humidity stay steady. That way the surface stays clean, and you avoid things like dust marks, uneven shine or moisture getting trapped on big fuselage areas

- Branding updates or large graphic applications for airlines, where wide and well-lit working space helps maintain consistency across tails, fuselages and engine covers

- Refinishing composite parts after bonded-structure repairs, keeping moisture under control so carbon-fiber components maintain strength before applying primer or topcoat

- Applying aerospace coatings such as anti-icing layers, erosion-resistant finishes or low-observability coatings, which all need stable temperature and humidity to cure correctly

- Recoating radomes and other areas that affect radar signals, where a clean booth prevents particles or contaminants from interfering with RF performance

- Applying corrosion-resistant primer on aluminum or composite parts works better in a controlled space, where the coating stays even and the drying doesn’t fluctuate

Risks of Painting an Aircraft Without a Dedicated Paint Booth

 Coating defects
 If the aircraft is painted outdoors, it’s easy for dust or little insects to fall on the surface before the paint dries. When that happens, the coating doesn’t adhere as it should, which increases the risk of corrosion over time. It may also affect how air moves around that section of the aircraft, even in smaller areas.

Safety concerns
 Aviation coatings are flammable, so they really need a safe workspace. If the working environment isn’t explosion proof or fireproof, the fumes can ignite with any spark or heat source, especially during spraying or while the paint is drying and releasing more solvent.

Environmental violations
 IIf the aircraft painting facility doesn’t control VOC emissions, the release of solvents directly into the air goes against ICAO environmental requirements. This can lead to penalties and extra inspections.

title: Marine & Yacht Paint Booth
content:
![Marine & Yacht Paint Booth](https://ysanona.com/products/1-4-marine-yacht-paint-booth_01.webp)

Marine & Yacht Paint Booth

Industrial spray booths for boat and marine finishing

Marine and yacht paint booths are built for coating work on hulls, hull sections, full yachts and large marine components. They keep the temperature, airflow and humidity steady, which helps the coating settle smoothly on fiberglass, aluminum and steel surfaces.

A sealed booth also keeps dust and moisture away during spraying and curing, which is crucial on wide hull panels. Bright and even lighting helps operators see how well the color is covering the surface, check the gloss level and spot small defects, especially on the long and curved sections that are very common in boats and yachts. These booths can be combined with other equipment of a marine coating line, such as powder coating rooms, curing ovens, wash bays, prep rooms, paint mixing rooms and storage for flammable materials, giving workshops a complete and organized setup for marine refinishing.

Design Considerations for Marine & Yacht Paint Booths

 Structural strength
 Marine booths often handle oversized parts, so the overall structure needs enough strength and rigidity to support the large dimensions and long spans.

Sealed door systems
 Doors such as electric lifting doors, folding doors or sliding doors must provide good sealing and resist wind pressure to keep dust, moisture and outside air from entering the booth.

Laminar airflow
 Many marine booths use a top-down airflow design, where clean air comes from the ceiling and is pulled out through the floor or lower side channels. The downward airflow helps carry the overspray away from the hull, keeping the coating cleaner and easier to control as the coating work is carried out.

Hot-air circulation system
 Stainless-steel heaters combined with corrosion-resistant ducting help avoid rust problems, especially in the high-heat and high-humidity conditions found in marine coating work.

Heavy-duty transfer carts or turntables
 These electric platforms allow large hull sections to rotate or move into position, helping operators reach different sides without blind spots.

High-access work platforms
 The upper parts of a hull are hard to reach because of the height and curvature. Lift platforms or elevated walkways allow operators to access those areas safely for spraying or checking the surface.

title: Woodworking Paint Booth
content:
![Woodworking Paint Booth](https://ysanona.com/products/1-6-woodworking-paint-booth_01.webp)

Woodworking Paint Booth

Industrial spray booths for wood cabinets and furniture finishing

Woodworking paint booths are commonly used in furniture shops and cabinet workshops where wood pieces need a clean, steady workspace for spraying. The booth helps keep dust away from freshly coated surfaces and gives operators enough room to move around doors, drawers, panels and other items without disturbing the finish. Lighting inside the booth is arranged to brighten the whole work area, especially the spots where wood grain, edges and panel joints are checked. This helps operators see how the varnish or color coat is settling on each piece.

Some workshops add a drying section when they need coatings to cure faster. The air in these drying rooms can be heated with gas, diesel, electricity or steam, depending on the setup of the shop. This helps speed up production while keeping the finish smooth and consistent across different types of wood.

Customized Airflow Types
 Different woodworking paint booth models use different airflow patterns, and the choice usually depends on budget, workshop layout or the operator’s preference.

Cross Draft Booth
 Fresh air enters through the filter at the front of the booth and then moves straight toward the exhaust unit at the back. The airflow runs directly from the front to the back, which makes this one of the simplest booth designs. It’s a practical option for smaller workshops that can’t spare much room for a larger setup.

Side Downdraft Booth
 Fresh air enters from the front of the booth and moves downward toward exhaust points on the rear wall or the side panels. With the airflow moving this way, overspray is pulled away more effectively and the coating lands more evenly on the wood.

Customer Case

YNZ Interior Co., Ltd. from Thailand purchased a YS-PB4 furniture paint booth for their furniture finishing projects. We arranged a technician to handle the paid installation, and the booth was fully set up in just three days. The customer told us they were happy with both the equipment and how smoothly the installation went.

Open Face Paint Booth

Industrial spray booths with open front for easy access and movement of large workpieces

Open face paint booths are used in workshops that need an open entry for spraying large or irregular workpieces. Instead of a fully enclosed structure, the front is open and the booth may have only partial walls or a simple canopy. The airflow pulls the overspray toward the filters at the back, which helps keep dust and paint mist from spreading. Open-face booths are commonly used in furniture workshops, small production lines and automotive parts finishing. The open front makes it easier for operators to move pieces in and out and work with items of different sizes without being limited by enclosed walls.

Applications

 ![Workpieces that do not require strict finishing conditions](https://ysanona.com/images/abouticon_01.webp)

Workpieces that do not require strict finishing conditions Used for primer and anti-rust coatings, as well as sections that do not require a high level of gloss or surface cleanliness.

 ![Small or temporary spraying tasks](https://ysanona.com/images/abouticon_04.webp)

Small or temporary spraying tasks Used for small projects or quick spraying tasks inside the workshop. It’s helpful when only a basic area is needed to apply a coat and a fully enclosed booth is not required.

 ![On-site repair work](https://ysanona.com/images/abouticon_02.webp)

On-site repair work Often used when large equipment or fixed structures can’t be moved into a closed spray booth. Thanks to the open layout, operators can handle small touch-ups or repair work directly on site without having to move the piece.

Advantages

- Open-face booths cost less because their structure is very simple. Most setups only use a light canopy, a small exhaust fan and a simple filter section. Because of that, the upfront cost stays much lower than in a fully enclosed booth.

- The open design provides more space to work with long, wide or tall pieces, since there are no fixed walls in the way. It is a practical option for oversized parts that cannot be placed inside a closed booth.

- Most of the airflow moves naturally, and a small exhaust fan is used only when extra extraction is needed. Energy use stays low, and the ventilation system is easy to maintain.

- The open front allows workpieces to move in and out easily, which is useful when using cranes, forklifts or other lifting equipment to position heavy or large items.

- Daily use is easier than in enclosed booths because there aren’t as many settings to manage. It is suitable for spraying tasks that don’t need very strict control of temperature or airflow.

Main Configuration

- Wall Panels
 The booth is built with 1.5 mm galvanized steel panels that are shaped and secured with screws. The structure is sturdy and easy to assemble, without needing anything too complex.

- Lighting Fixtures
 The YS-OF2 comes with one light fixture, while the YS-OF3 includes two. Each fixture has two 36-watt explosion-proof tubes that provide steady, reliable illumination for spraying operations every day.

- Exhaust System
 An explosion-proof axial fan handles the air removal and helps keep overspray and fumes from building up in the work area. This adds safety when working with solvent-based coatings.

title: Retractable Paint Booth
content:
![Retractable Paint Booth](https://ysanona.com/products/1-9-retractable-paint-booth_01.webp)

Retractable Paint Booth

Portable spray booths with retractable enclosure for painting hard-to-move workpieces

Retractable paint booths use a sliding metal frame and a PVC-coated enclosure to form a temporary area for spraying. The structure extends to create a closed workspace and folds back after the painting work is finished, leaving the floor space available again. It is a suitable option for workshops that deal with heavy or large parts, since the booth can be moved to the workpiece instead of relocating the part.

Advantages
 Compared with fixed paint booths, retractable paint booths offer several the following advantages :

- The initial investment is lower because the frame and enclosure cost less than insulated wall panels, and no civil construction is required.

- Floor space is used more efficiently. The booth expands only during spraying and stays folded the rest of the time, which helps save valuable workshop area and reduces rental or construction costs. This is especially helpful for facilities with limited space, such as dealerships or repair shops in urban areas.

- Different workpiece sizes can be handled, as the length, width and height are usually customizable or adjustable through multiple retractable sections. This allows the structure to fit anything from passenger cars to large construction equipment.

- It adapts to various site conditions. It can be installed in a standard workshop, in buildings with limited height, or even in semi-outdoor areas as long as the ground is reinforced.

- One workstation can be used for different tasks. The same area can switch between repair work, bodywork or painting, improving how the space is used throughout the day.

Customer Case

A retractable paint booth was customized for a large workpiece used in mineral power stations in Chile. The workpiece the client needed to coat had a maximum length of 60m and cannot be moved, so a retractable structure was required to meet the painting process on site.

The paint booth was designed with an inner size of 10 m × 7 m × 5.6 m, which fits a workpiece measuring 5 m in width and 4.5 m in height. The exhaust extraction system could move freely along both sides and was usually positioned on the left or right depending on the spraying area. It was equipped with a 15 kW motor that provided an air velocity of around 0.3 m/s at the extraction surface. Fiberglass filters and filter paper were used to capture overspray, and activated carbon helped reduce paint odor.

A fully enclosed configuration can also be supplied for projects that need a more controlled environment. This option adds full sidewalls and roof panels to create a closed spraying space, improving overspray control and airflow management when required.

title: Paint Drying Room
content:
![Paint Drying Room](https://ysanona.com/products/3-2-paint-drying-room_01.webp)

Paint Drying Room

Waterborne paint drying booth designed for shortening the drying time of water-based paints

The waterborne paint drying system is engineered to reduce the drying time of water-based coatings or paint.

The drying system is used throughout both spraying and baking stages. During spraying, fast downward airflow moves air across the vehicle and into the extraction unit, helping waterborne coatings begin to flash off quickly. During baking, heated air reaches the painted surface more directly, drawing out moisture and fumes at a faster rate. This combination shortens curing time for automotive repairs and increases overall shop productivity.

Why Choose Yokistar Waterborne Paint Drying Systems?

Waterborne paint spray booths are developed to help water-based coatings achieve their best possible finish. These coatings continue to gain popularity because they are more environmentally friendly than solvent-based products.

Although application steps are similar, water-based paints have higher viscosity and behave differently once sprayed. Solvent-based coats typically reach 2–3 mil in thickness across three layers, while three layers of water-based coatings are roughly half that. This thinner coverage works well for multi-color designs or striped finishes, but it also highlights surface flaws more easily.

Yokistar waterborne paint booths maintain a clean and controlled environment to protect the surface from dust and contaminants, helping the final finish remain smooth and uniform. A high-efficiency filtration system prevents particles from entering the airstream, while temperature and humidity inside the cabin can be adjusted to support correct application and fast curing.

As the regulations in many regions, including parts of the United States tighten, waterborne coatings are becoming the preferred choice as they dry through evaporation and are one of the most eco-friendly options. Installing a waterborne paint booth ensures your shop is prepared for future restrictions on solvent-based products, while reducing environmental impact and improving operational efficiency.

Each of our waterborne paint booth is tested for durability and performance, requiring minimal maintenance and delivering reliable results across daily use.

title: FAQs
content:
FAQs

What types of paint booths are available?

The range includes spray booths used in automotive repair, bus and truck workshops, aircraft coating, marine and yacht finishing, industrial equipment painting and furniture coating. It also covers open-face booths for basic spraying tasks, tabletop booths for small parts, retractable booths for tight spaces and spray & bake booths designed for water-based paint. Each paint booth type is designed to match different workpiece sizes and coating requirements.

How does a paint booth work?

A paint booth works by pulling in filtered air and moving it across the area where the coating is applied, so the overspray is taken away and dust can’t settle on the surface. The airflow can be vertical or horizontal, depending on the booth design, and helps maintain a clean environment by preventing dust and other particles from entering the coating area. Some models also offer temperature and humidity control to support proper paint application and curing. Depending on the configuration, the booth may handle only spraying operations or combine spraying and drying functions in the same enclosure.

How does an automotive paint booth keep the spraying environment clean?

Automotive paint booths rely on a sealed structure and a multi-layer filtration setup that stops dust and other small particles before they can enter the spraying area. The airflow moves in a controlled direction, creating a barrier that keeps outside contaminants, such as fine dust, floating particles or even small insects, away from the coating surface. This controlled setup keeps the spraying area clean throughout the whole process, so the paint goes on evenly and the surface isn’t affected by dust or small particles.

What are the practical differences between Yubase-O, Yubase-B and Yubase-T?

Yubase-O uses two 3 kW motors installed inside the cabin together with dual-inlet centrifugal fans. Having the motors inside keeps the design simple and helps the airflow stay steady while the booth is running.
 Yubase-B also uses two 3 kW motors with dual-inlet centrifugal fans, but it runs with a belt-drive system. Since the belts and pulleys are exposed and simple to access, it's easier to carry out regular maintenance and any replacement can be handled quickly.
 Yubase-T works a little differently from the other Yubase models. It runs with one 7.5 kW motor that drives a turbine fan directly, which gives it a stronger airflow. Because of this fan setup, the outside layout looks a bit different compared with the other models. Since there’s no belt in between, the power goes straight to the fan, so the airflow responds quicker when the booth is running.

What heating options are available for the bus and truck paint booths? Can they meet drying requirements for large vehicles?

Bus and truck paint booths can be equipped with gas, diesel or electric heating, either as a single option or as a mixed setup. For example, a typical configuration may use a 232 kW stainless-steel heat exchanger together with a Riello RG5S diesel burner rated at 260,000 kcal/h. The heating capacity is enough for curing buses and trucks, incluso when the surfaces are large or the coating layer is thicker.

Do the bus and truck spray booths meet environmental and safety requirements?

These booths follow EN779 filtration standards and can use filters such as G3 and F5. They can also be fitted with activated-carbon adsorption and RCO (regenerative catalytic oxidation) systems, giving VOC removal rates above 90%. For safety, the design includes explosion-proof components, gas detectors and a linked CO₂ fire-suppression system. All these features help the booth meet environmental requirements and maintain safe operation throughout the process.

How accurate is the temperature control in bus and truck paint booths, and does it affect the coating quality?

The booth uses an automatic temperature control system with an accuracy of about ±2°C. Maintaining a steady temperature is important as it allows the coating cure evenly, so the paint adheres better and dries smoothly, without any noticeable issues. When the heat fluctuates too much, large panels can develop cracks or uneven areas, so this level of control plays an important role in maintaining a smooth and consistent finish on buses and trucks.

What auxiliary equipment can be added to improve spraying results on buses and trucks with irregular shapes?

Depending on the structure of the vehicle, the booth can be fitted with tools that help reach difficult areas. Options include a rotating platform or a lifting platform for full-angle access, and a custom three-axis movable platform that allows operators to work comfortably on side panels, roof sections and other areas where large vehicles usually need extra coverage.

Is the control system in the bus and truck spray booths easy to use, and does it support remote management?

Bus and truck spray booths come with a control system that includes a temperature controller, baking timer, switches and fault indicators. The control panel uses a PLC with a touch screen, so the operator can set the temperature, airflow and curing time directly from the screen. It also has a remote-monitoring option, which lets technicians see how the booth is running, get fault alerts right away and adjust basic settings even if they’re not in the workshop.

For workshops with restricted space, are there bus & truck spray booth options designed to fit more compact layouts?

Yes. For workshops that don’t have enough indoor space, there are outdoor paint booths built with a protective outer shell to handle harsh weather. Some models can also be made in a split structure, where the main cabin is divided into two sections using folding doors or an electric roller door. This layout helps fit the booth into tighter areas while still keeping enough room for bus and truck coating work.

What does the aircraft paint booth do to help with small-area repairs on the fuselage, such as lightning-strike marks or corrosion damage?

Paint booths keep the repair area clean by stopping dust and other particles from getting into the coating. They also let users control the temperature very precisely, which helps the paint cure evenly and settle well on the repaired spot. As a result, the repaired area ends up matching the surrounding finish, making the transition hard to notice.

How does the aircraft paint booth prevent defects such as particles or bubbles when the entire aircraft is being repainted?

When the whole aircraft needs a new coat, the booth keeps the temperature and humidity inside at a steady level, so outside conditions don’t interfere with the coating process. At the same time, the filtration system captures dust and other airborne particles before they reach the surface. Keeping a stable and clean environment from the beginning makes it much easier to avoid problems like dust settling on the surface, bubbles forming in the coating or areas that dry unevenly.

How do the aircraft spray cabins maintain bonding strength when coating repaired carbon-fiber parts?

These aircraft spray cabins keep the humidity under tight control, which matters a lot when working with carbon-fiber repairs. If moisture gets between the coating and the carbon-fiber surface, the bond weakens quickly. By keeping the air dry and stable, the coating sticks the way it should and the repaired parts keep their strength.

How do the paint booths help extend the service life of aluminum-alloy parts when applying an anti-corrosion primer?

The coating cabins allow the primer to be applied at a steady, correct thickness, and the curing area maintains stable temperature and humidity while it dries. This helps the primer settle well on the aluminum surface and form a stronger bond. Once the primer dries completely, it forms a stronger protective film against corrosion, so the aluminum parts stay in good condition for a longer time.

Can these spray paint booths support the weight of yachts or other large marine components?

Yes. These marine spray cabins are built with reinforced structures that can handle oversized components, including full hulls, hull sections and complete yacht bodies. They’re built to handle heavy loads, so the structure can safely carry large hulls or yacht sections while the coating work is done. There’s no risk of bending or instability during spraying or curing.

How does the airflow design in marine paint booths ensure that overspray is removed effectively without affecting the coating quality?

These marine spray paint booths use top pressure-balanced supply air together with pit exhaust or lower-side exhaust. This creates a vertical laminar airflow that moves straight down through the workspace. With this steady airflow, the overspray is pushed out quickly and doesn’t stay floating around the hull, which helps keep the coating clean and even.

Are the door options convenient for moving yachts and large ship sections in and out of the paint booth, and do they keep the booths well sealed?

Yes, marine coating booths can be equipped with electric lift doors, folding doors or sliding doors. All of them make it easier to move full yacht bodies or large sections in and out. At the same time, the doors seal well and resist strong wind pressure, so the coating environment inside stays stable.

How does the hot-air circulation system prevent corrosion when the paint booths are used in high-humidity environments?

The hot-air circulation system uses stainless-steel heaters and corrosion-resistant air ducts. These materials hold up well against heat and moisture, so they don’t rust even when the spray booth is running in high-humidity marine environments. This keeps the system in good working condition for longer and helps the airflow stay stable during operation.

What equipment can support full-coverage spraying when working with yacht hulls or segmented ship sections?

These marine spray booths can be fitted with heavy-duty transfer carts or large rotating platforms that move or turn hull sections using an electric drive system. They can also be combined with height-adjustable work platforms, so operators can reach upper areas comfortably. With both systems working together, it becomes easier to spray every angle of the hull or section without leaving untreated spots.

Apart from marine spray and drying booths, what other equipment is available to support the full coating process?

Marine coating projects often require several supporting units to complete each stage of the workflow. The available options include powder-coating rooms, curing ovens, washing rooms, preparation rooms, paint-mixing rooms and dedicated storage spaces for hazardous materials. With these additional units, the coating process, from surface preparation to the final coating steps, can be carried out in a structured and continuous way.

Can the lighting system in the marine spray booths meet the requirements for coating complex hull structures?

The lighting setup used in these marine spray booths is designed to illuminate the cabin evenly, giving operators aclear view of every surface. It provides strong, diffused light that reaches corners, curved areas and higher sections of the hull. The clear and even lighting helps operators see every detail of the hull during spraying. Corners, curved areas and higher sections become easier to inspect, so the coating can be applied with better control and a more consistent finish.

Can the spray booths be customized for large industrial equipment, such as heavy machinery or oversized enclosures?

Yes. These large-format spray booths can be tailored to match the needs of different industrial projects. The available options include downdraft layouts, dry-filter designs and booths built specifically for trucks and buses. These setups work well with different kinds of heavy equipment and large structures, so the booth can handle many types of coating jobs without problem.

How does the ventilation system in industrial equipment spray booths balance filtration performance and operating costs?

The ventilation system in these industrial spray booths uses high-efficiency filter media designed to handle heat, dust and paint particles. The filters have a large surface area and a progressive-density structure, which helps them trap more contaminants while keeping airflow resistance low. Since the filters last longer and do not need to be replaced as often, the overall operating cost stays lower during daily use.

Can the lighting system meet the visibility requirements for coating large industrial equipment with complex shapes?

Yes. The industrial spray booths use Philips tri-phosphor tubes installed in the plenum and side walls, protected by tempered-glass covers. This setup provides bright and stable lighting across the entire workspace. Even corners, recessed areas and higher sections of large equipment can be seen clearly. Optional external maintenance walkways are also available, making the lighting easier to service while maintaining accurate spraying conditions.

Is the packaging suitable for long-distance transportation to ensure the booth components arrive intact?

For long-distance shipping, the spray booth components are wrapped with bubble film to absorb bumps and vibration during the journey. If the project requires extra protection, they can also be packed on reinforced steel pallets. These options help keep every part safe and prevent damage while the equipment is being transported.

What types of furniture can be coated in the woodworking paint booth?

The woodworking paint booth is designed for wooden products of different sizes, so it works well for chairs, tables, cabinet doors and similar pieces. It supports clear coats, glossy finishes, primers and topcoats, all in a clean and bright space that helps keep dust away and gives the operator better visibility while spraying.

Is there a way to reduce the drying time for wooden furniture?

Yes. When faster drying is needed, a drying room can be added to the setup. The air inside can be heated with gas, diesel, electricity or steam, which helps wood furniture and other wood products cure more quickly and consistently.

What airflow options are available for the woodworking paint booth with drying section, and how are they different?

Woodworking paint booths with drying sections can be designed with two airflow layouts: crossflow or side-down draft. With the crossflow airflow design fresh air enters through the filters at the front and moves straight across the booth toward the exhaust section at the back. In the side-down draft airflow layout the air also enters from the front, but it gets pulled down or sideways depending on where the exhaust units are placed (back wall, side wall or lower section).
 Both options handle overspray without problems. The main difference is the way air moves inside the booth. In a crossflow layout, the air travels straight from the front to the back. In a side-down draft layout, the air gets pulled down or to the sides, so the spray area stays clearer and more even, especially when working with wider or taller wooden panels.

Does choosing between crossflow and side-down draft airflow affect coating efficiency in woodworking paint booths?

Crossflow and side-down draft mainly change how the overspray moves inside the booth. Crossflow pushes the air straight from the front toward the back, while the side-down draft pulls the air downward and out through the lower or side exhaust zones. Both options handle overspray well once they’re set up according to the workshop layout. In day-to-day use, the difference in coating speed or efficiency is minimal, so most customers just pick the airflow pattern that fits their space and the way they usually work.

What types of workpieces are suitable for open paint booths?

Open-type paint booths are suitable for tasks where the coating process doesn’t require a fully controlled environment. Ideal for priming, anti-rust coatings and projects where the final surface doesn’t need a high-gloss look or a perfectly clean finish. They’re also practical for temporary jobs, small-batch spraying, or on-site touch-ups of large equipment.

Compared with enclosed paint booths, what cost advantages do open face paint booths offer?

Open face paint booths are much more affordable to build, install and maintain. These booths use a simple structure with a canopy, an exhaust fan and basic filters, which keeps the initial cost low. They use less energy too, since most of the airflow comes from natural ventilation and the exhaust fan doesn’t need a high-power motor. For workshops that need to keep costs under control, they’re a more affordable option compared to enclosed booths.

Can open paint booths handle oversized workpieces?

Yes. Open face paint booths can handle very large components because there are no fixed walls limiting the space. The open face layout gives enough room for extra-long, extra-wide or extra-tall parts, and moving them in and out is much easier compared to enclosed structures.

Are there any special site requirements when installing an open face paint booth?

The site requirements are minimal. There’s no need for a fixed building or a dedicated room, which makes installation flexible. An open paint booth can be placed in a free area inside the workshop or on a flat outdoor space. What really matters is having enough room to fit the workpieces comfortably and making sure there’s simple airflow so the overspray can move out of the working area without building up.

What types of small workpieces can be coated in tabletop spray bake booths?

Tabletop spray-bake booths are a good match for small parts made of wood, metal, plastic and other similar materials. They offer a clean, well-lit workspace where it’s easier to handle different shapes and get an even, predictable finish.

How do table top paint booths keep the workspace ventilated and reduce overspray buildup?

Table top paint booths pull fresh air in through the front opening, moves it across the work area and sends it out through the filter section at the back. With the explosion-proof axial fan keeping the airflow steady, the booth stays well-ventilated and overspray doesn’t settle in the workspace.

How do table top paint booths manage uneven or irregular small parts to achieve a uniform finish?

Table top paint booths come with a 360° rotating worktable that lets the part move smoothly while spraying. This rotation lets the operator reach all sides comfortably, without having to stop and move the part over and over. Even pieces with awkward or uneven shapes end up getting sprayed from every angle, so the coating looks even and complete.

Can the filtration system in a table top paint booth effectively remove overspray to prevent contamination?

The filtration setup in table top paint booths uses an angled fiberglass filter that captures the overspray as it moves through the airflow path. This helps keep paint particles from spreading into the workshop and protects the ventilation components behind the filter. By catching the overspray right away, the booth keeps the area cleaner and helps prevent paint mist from collecting on nearby parts of the equipment.

Can a table top paint booth be customized if the standard sizes don’t fit certain small parts?

If the standard table top paint booth models are too small or too big for the customer’s workpieces, the paint booth can be made in custom dimensions. That way, the booth will match the size of the parts, and the coating work can be done without struggling for space.

In workshops or 4S service centers with limited space, how does a retractable paint booth help make better use of the area?

A retractable paint booth is helpful in tight spaces because it doesn’t stay open all the time. It’s only extended while spraying, and once the job is done, it folds back so the space can be used for other tasks. That means the booth doesn’t occupy the space permanently, and the workshop can move things around and use the area more freely for everyday work.

Can a retractable paint booth handle different workpiece sizes, from passenger cars to large construction machinery?

A retractable paint booth can be built with custom length, width and height, or designed with multiple sliding sections. With these adjustments, the structure can open wide for large industrial equipment or stay smaller for passenger-car jobs. With those adjustments, a single booth can handle all kinds of workpieces, so there’s no need to prepare separate areas for different sizes.

Can a retractable paint booth be used for multiple workstations, and does this help improve space efficiency?

A retractable paint booth can be shared across different types of work. The same area can switch between repair work, body panel preparation or coating tasks, depending on what the workshop needs at the moment. This reduces the downtime of single-purpose stations and helps the workshop make better use of limited space.

What is the purpose of connecting the paint mixing bench to the ventilation system of the mixing room?

When the paint mixing bench is linked to the ventilation system, the fumes produced during paint preparation are removed quickly. The bench also includes a sink for cleaning spray guns and storage space for materials, which makes the setup safer and more convenient during daily work.

What factors are considered when customizing a paint preparation station to support coating quality?

Custom paint prep stations are planned around the specific needs of the coating process. Key points include filtration, lighting, temperature and airflow, so each step before spraying takes place under suitable conditions. By meeting these requirements, the station helps maintain consistent quality in the following coating steps.

What advantages do infrared curing lamps offer in mobility and heat efficiency?

The infrared curing lamps use 304 stainless-steel reflector panels, which direct more heat toward the workpiece and improve energy use. They also come with smooth-rolling casters, so the lamps can move easily between the workshop, the spray booth and the prep area. The lamp can be moved right up to the area that needs heat, so the curing stays consistent no matter which workstation is being used.

How does a water-based paint booth ensure a proper finish when using waterborne coatings?

A water-based paint booth includes a filtration system that keeps dust and particles out of the airflow, preventing contamination on the painted surface. The booth can also control temperature and humidity, which helps waterborne coatings level out, dry faster and cure evenly. When the booth keeps the temperature and humidity steady, water-based coatings settle more evenly, so the surface looks smoother and small flaws are less noticeable.

Why is an automotive frame straightening machine necessary when repairing collision damage?

A collision can distort the vehicle’s structure, affect chassis alignment and interfere with the correct positioning of key components. A frame straightening machine restores the damaged sections to their original dimensions and brings the reference points of the chassis back to specification. If this step is skipped, the vehicle won’t recover the strength it needs, and it won’t be safe to drive again.
